text: The surgeon begins the robotic ventral hernia repair by placing trocars, starting at Palmer's Point in the left upper quadrant beneath the rib cage, and positioning the camera port laterally for optimal overview. An 8mm trocar is placed in the left lower quadrant. Using robotic scissors, the surgeon performs dissection to establish a landing zone for the mesh, applying traction and counter traction while preserving the peritoneum. The edges of the fascia are scored to aid tissue adhesion, and a halved ruler is used for mesh size identification.

The surgeon uses a Stratafix absorbable number one PDS suture in a running fashion to re-approximate tissue during the robotic ventral hernia repair. The suture is gently cinched down in a corset-like manner to close the defect, avoiding excessive grabbing of the suture and instead using the needle driver tips for advancement. The repair is performed in a double-layer fashion to ensure closure of the large defect.

The assistant palpates the hernia defect to ensure no fat or tissue is incarcerated before closure. The surgeon confirms the defect is closed and measures it. A custom-cut piece of polypropylene mesh is prepared with at least four to five centimeters of overlap on each side. The surgeon creates a pocket for the mesh and uses a double-arm PDS suture to secure it, ensuring the mesh is in place and the peritoneum is re-approximated. The suture is tied to itself, and the other arm is used to complete the peritoneal closure.

The surgeon uses robotic articulation to take precise bites of the suture, addressing a small defect with incarcerated omentum visible. The peritoneum is re-approximated, and the omentum is used to cover the mesh, ensuring no direct contact with the uncoated mesh.

The surgeon inspects the surgical site to ensure the mesh and hernia defect are completely covered. A vacuum dressing is applied over the defect with a four-by-four gauze to prevent seroma formation. The air is suctioned out using a needle.
